body: The Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board (JAMB) was established by Decree No. 2 of 1978. It was created to conduct entrance examinations for tertiary institutions in Nigeria and regulate admissions into universities, polytechnics and colleges of education.

As of 2025, Nigeria has over 170 accredited universities — federal, state and private. This makes Nigeria one of the countries with the highest number of universities in Africa. The National Universities Commission (NUC) regulates all universities in the country.

The West African Examinations Council (WAEC) was established in 1952 and began conducting examinations in 1953. It serves five West African countries: Nigeria, Ghana, Sierra Leone, The Gambia, and Liberia. The Senior School Certificate Examination (SSCE) is its most popular exam in Nigeria.

fact: First Class is the highest university degree classification in Nigeria
body: In Nigerian universities using the 5.0 GPA scale, a First Class degree requires a minimum CGPA of 4.50. Second Class Upper (2:1) starts at 3.50, Second Class Lower (2:2) at 2.40, Third Class at 1.50 and Pass at 1.00. Students below 1.00 fail the degree programme.

fact: NELFUND gives interest-free loans to Nigerian students
body: The Nigerian Education Loan Fund (NELFUND) was launched in 2024 to provide interest-free loans to students in federal and state tertiary institutions. Eligible students can access funds for tuition and upkeep. Repayment begins 2 years after graduation or when the student gets a job — whichever comes first.
